text: This is a case of a laparoscopic chordate lobectomy in a gentleman with Abernathy malformation for a large adenoma associated with this problem. His preoperative workup was otherwise unremarkable. He'd had a rapid increase in the size of one of his adenomas in the chordate lobe seen here on the MRI scan in close proximity to the shunt.

The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical port for laparoscopy, with two additional 12mm ports and a sub-xiphoid incision for a liver retractor. The operation begins by dissecting the ligamentum teres and falciform ligament to retract the left lobe of the liver. The left triangular ligament is dissected up to the inferior vena cava, allowing the liver to become mobile enough for placement of a Nathanson liver retractor.

The surgeon exposes the veil of tissue overlying the enlarged caudate lobe, with the replaced left hepatic artery visibly flopping over the top. The decision is made to leave the aberrant left hepatic artery in situ due to the abnormal anatomy.

The surgeon performs dissection around the capsule of the liver and the chordate lobe, progressing towards the base of the lesser omentum. Large accessory vessels are visible, and the Thunderbeat device is used for its harmonic and bipolar capabilities, facilitating blood sealing and rapid dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection behind the insertion of the left hepatic vein into the inferior vena cava, proceeding down along the front of the caudate lobe where it joins the left lobe of the liver in the groove for the ligamentum venosum. Areolar tissue is further dissected around the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon extends the dissection of fibrous tissue between the chordate lobe and the left lateral section to achieve a clear medial margin. The portacaval shunt is visible below the chordate lobe, with a tongue of chordate lobe tissue encircling it. The inferior vena cava comes into view, highlighting the advantages of laparoscopic surgery in this area.

The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to control a small bleed from chordate vessels on the inferior vena cava. Further dissection reveals short gastric and hepatic vessels traveling from the chordate lobe into the inferior vena cava, which are secured with blocking haemoclips.

The laparoscopic view reveals the length of the inferior vena cava, allowing identification of small vessels. These vessels are clipped after dissection, while the caudate side is controlled with bipolar diathermy before cutting to minimize clip usage and avoid clip traffic.

The surgeon divides small tributaries from the hilum to the caudate lobe using bipolar diathermy, targeting vessels feeding the adenoma visible as a large expansion of the caudate lobe. Dissection continues on the undersurface of the replaced hepatic artery within the lesser omentum, controlling and dissecting larger vessels from the portal venous structures in the hilum.

The surgeon identifies the main feeding vein and a small torn artery at the back, using a right angle instrument for dissection. The torn artery causes minimal bleeding, which is controlled with bipolar diathermy. The decision is made to leave the closely applied shunt in place near the chordate lobe.

The surgeon dissects alongside the shunt, identifying small tributaries running to the caudate lobe. These vessels are coagulated with bipolar diathermy and cut using the harmonic function of the Thunderbeak. Blunt dissection is employed to develop tissue planes, minimizing parenchymal destruction.

The surgery is being performed using an Olympus three-dimensional laparoscope with glasses that give three-dimensional type view. It's a very comfortable way of observing what's going on and in some ways may help with the dissection and recognition of structures and certainly helps with suturing and a feeling of depth perception that can only be gained through three-D imaging. The other advantage of the three-dimensional scope for this type of an operation is that it does have a great deal of flexibility once it's inside the abdomen. It can turn around almost to ninety degrees allowing the camera to be placed out of the way of the instruments and yet give a great view.

The surgeon continues dissection along the inferior vena cava, identifying two or three substantial tributaries from the chordate lobe that require formal ligation. The inferior vena cava is carefully dissected away from the posterior aspect of the chordate lobe.

The surgeon attempts to navigate around the bridge of caudate tissue over the shunt leading to the inferior vena cava. A tape is passed from behind to visualize the front, but the attempt is unsuccessful. Further dissection is performed from the front towards the vena cava, with a small branch from the shunt to the caudate lobe being dissected to elongate the shunt. Anterior tape is passed to check visibility from the posterior aspect, revealing a developing window of areolar tissue to the left of the Thunderbeak device. The tape indicates the shunt's position below this point.

The surgeon uses tape for traction while carefully dissecting near the chordate lobe to control inflow and separate it from vital structures. The dissection focuses on finding the correct plane outside the parenchyma of the chordate and left lobe of the liver, avoiding premature division of the tissue bridge to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon identifies a small arterial bleeder from the back of the hepatic artery, preserving the main left hepatic artery. Dissection continues into the bridge of liver tissue as the chordate lobe crosses over to the main liver, ensuring an adequate margin through the parenchyma. A venous bleeder is packed off with a Raytech to control bleeding. The chordate lobe appears ischemic, displaying a pale color indicating loss of blood flow.

The surgeon performs dissection in front of the inferior vena cava and behind the left hepatic vein, revealing the common trunk where the middle hepatic vein joins the left hepatic vein before inserting into the cava. Using a laparoscope, the surgeon safely navigates this area, dividing the superior attachments of the caudate lobe while preserving the replaced hepatic artery.

The surgeon uses the Thunder Pete device as a dissector and vessel sealer to take down the bridge of tissue over the front of the shunt. The view is clear after cleaning the camera, revealing a small vein that is addressed with bipolar cautery.

The surgeon carefully places tape to avoid damaging the underlying shunt, allowing elevation of the caudate lobe at a different angle to approach its remaining attachments in front of the inferior vena cava. Small vessels are visible between the porta hepatis and the caudate lobe, with the hepatic artery posing a minor obstruction. A suction device is used to manage minor oozing from the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a sucker as a retractor and blunt dissector alongside the Thunderbead energy device to manipulate a thin bridge of tissue running in front of the inferior vena cava. The mobilization of the cava continues, allowing the bridge to be taken with a stapling device. The shunt has fallen away from the working area, facilitating the division of the bridge.

A small vessel is controlled by placing a tie around it, followed by the application of a Hemlock clip. The opposite side of the vessel is sealed using the Thunder Beat device, ensuring effective hemostasis in the limited space.

The surgeon is positioned underneath the caudate lobe, carefully dissecting the last attachments towards the porta hepatis. A stapler is used to secure the tissue, ensuring no clips are caught inside to prevent misfire. The VATEC is pulled out of the way to avoid interference during stapling.

The surgeon divides a capsule at the back of the caudate lobe, thinning the tissue further. The inferior vena cava is visible, bouncing at the back, while an artery is slung over the area. A fibrous structure resembling a small vessel is clipped with a hemlock clip. The caudate lobe is detached, and the hepatic artery remains patent. A bleeding point on the parenchymal surface is packed temporarily. The specimen is placed in a bag and removed through a small incision. After closing the incision, the packing is removed to address the bleeding area.

The surgeon sutures a bleeding point deep within the surgical site, using a needle to cinch the suture around the area. Despite the appearance of blood, the bleeding is minimal. Irrigation is performed, and fibrillar is applied to aid clot formation. The rest of the liver appears abnormal, and additional fibrillar is placed near the inferior vena cava.
